Direct land use change was not included for land use on the dairy farm, but instead soil carbon balance was modelled based on annual land use and land use practices. Indirect land use change emission was excluded.
2.2. Life cycle inventory analysis
2.2.1. Data collection

2.2.1.1. Dairy farm processes. Life cycle inventory data was collected on an annual basis for the average Dutch dairy farm for 1990 to 2019, based on Dutch national average dairy farm characteristics in terms of struc- tural variables (i.e. farm size, number of animals, milk production, ni- trogen excretion). The full dataset can be found in the supplementary information of this paper. An overview of the main dairy farm charac- teristics can be found in Table 1.

The average herd composition was calculated from the number of dairy cattle per animal category present in the Netherlands (van Brug- gen et al., 2021) and the number of dairy farms present (CBS, 2022a). The number of sold animals was calculated from the annual replacement rate of dairy cows and the number of calves born per cow per year (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). All youngstock that was born on farm but was not needed for replacement was assumed to be sold. Milk production per cow, including fat and protein content were taken from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) production was
Table 1
Development of main farm characteristics of an average Dutch dairy farm be- tween 1990 and 2019 (CBS, 2022a; van Bruggen et al., 2021; van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020; Wageningen UR, 2022).
a Calculated. b DMI = Dry matter intake.

calculated according to the formula recommended by IDF (IDF, 2015). Feed intake of the dairy herd was calculated from feed intake per animal category and feed type from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020) combined with animal numbers per an- imal category. The feed ration was composed of seven feed types: 1. Fresh grass, 2 grass silage, 3. Maize silage, 4. Co-products, 5. Low pro- tein compound feed, 6. High protein compound feed and 7. Whole milk (for youngstock younger than one year).

Enteric methane emission was calculated from methane emission factors per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and the number of animals per animal category present on farm. For each animal category the share of manure excreted in the slurry or solid manure management system was determined. Methane emission from manure storage was calculated from volatile solids excretion per animal category and the corresponding methane conversion factor for the manure management system. Direct nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calcu- lated from N excretion per animal category in the stable and the weighted emission factor for direct nitrous oxide corresponding with the share of manure excreted in the manure management system. Indirect nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calculated from the percentage of total ammoniacal nitrogen excreted per animal category and nitrogen excretion per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021).

The total area of grassland and maize land used on farm were derived from total mass of dry matter fed to the entire dairy herd, including yield and feed losses (van Dijk et al., 2021) and dry matter yields per hectare from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Soil type distinguished as mineral soil, peaty soil and soil were collected (Arets et al., 2019). Total nitrogen application from manure and artificial fertilizer per hectare were collected from FADN (Wage- ningen UR, 2022). FADN does not distinguish between N application rates on grassland and maize land. Therefore, the same nitrogen appli- cation rate for manure and artificial fertilizer for both grassland and maize land was assumed. Direct nitrous oxide emission from fertilization was calculated from nitrogen application rates for manure and artificial fertilizer and country specific emission factors (Van Dijk et al., 2021; Ruyssenaars et al., 2021) specified per soil type and type of nitrogen applied. For both peaty soils and peat soils the nitrous oxide emission factors for soils were applied.

Manure excreted in the pasture was calculated from N excretion in the pasture per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and animal numbers per animal category. Direct nitrous oxide emissions from manure excretion were calculated from nitrogen excreted in the pasture and a country specific emission factor specified per soil type.

Carbon dioxide and nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was quantified following the approach from Ruyssenaars et al. (2021), using Dutch emission factors based on the level of drainage and organic soil type. A distinction is made between peat soils, having more than 40 cm organic soil layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil, and peaty soils, having a 5-40 cm organic layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil. The share of grassland on organic soils for dairy farming was assumed to be the same as the share of the total area of grassland on organic soils present in the Netherlands, which is derived from Ruys- senaars et al. (2021). The organic soil area on the dairy farm is slightly decreasing over time, because the organic soils area in the Netherlands is oxidating. Nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was calculated based on an emission factor of 4.67 kg N2O-N.kg for peat soils and 4.09 kg N2O-N.kg ha for peaty soils (van der Zee et al., 2021).

The development of SOC stocks in mineral soils was simulated with the soil carbon model RothC (Coleman et al., 1997; Coleman and Jen- kinson, 2014). This is a dynamic model for the conversion of organic carbon in mineral soils. The model considers the effects of soil type, temperature, moisture content and soil cover on the decomposition of organic carbon. The resulting changes in SOC stocks were converted to CO2 emission or carbon sequestration for the CF. Monthly climate data for the period 1990-2019 was derived for 14 climate zones from the

national weather institute (KNMI). The starting point for SOC stocks was based on a national soil survey from the period 1995-2002 (Visschers et al., 2007). Changes in SOC stocks were simulated for permanent grassland (at least 5 years continuous grassland (CBS, 2022b)), tempo- rary grassland and maize land on sandy and clay soils and a soil depth of 30 cm. Carbon inputs were determined for crop residues and cover crops, manure and compost. Changes in the share of each land use, the type and amount of manure and the application of cover crops together with the climate determined the changes in SOC stocks over time.
2.2.2. Purchased resources

The CF of artificial fertilizers applied and produced in Europe was retrieved from published sources (Brentrup et al., 2016, 2018) for the years 1990, 2006, 2011 and 2014 in the time series and interpolated for intermediate years in the series, between 2014 and 2019 the CF was assumed stable. The CF takes into account the technological changes that occurred during the time series. Since the Netherlands is and has been a net exporter of fertilizer (De Haas and Van Dijk, 2011) all fer- tilizers were assumed to come from the Netherlands with a default transport distance to the dairy farm of 100 km. Details on assumptions and calculations are in Appendix A.

GHG emission from combustion of fuels for electricity production in the Netherlands was estimated for each year by dividing total GHG emission from the electricity sector in the Netherlands by the net elec- tricity produced in the Netherlands (CBS, 2022a). This approach is a simplified version of the "integral approach" as described by Harmelink et al. (2012), which is the basis for national statistics reported by the Central Bureau for Statistics (CBS). However, since data on GHG emis- sion from combustion for 1990-1999 was not available, a simplified method was used to calculate GHG emission from combustion per kilowatt-hour for all years. Appendix C shows a comparison between values used for this study and values from (Harmelink et al., 2012) and CBS. To determine the CF of electricity 5% additional GHG emission was assumed to account for upstream emissions (e.g. mining and transport of fossil fuels) following Edenhofer et al. (2012) and Harmelink et al. (2012).

Compound feed formulations are typically considered as confidential information by the feed industry. Therefore, it is difficult to obtain primary information on compound feed formulation, especially over a period of 30 years. Schothorst Feed Research analyses the market for compound feed ingredients and uses prices and nutritional requirements to optimize feed formulations for different dairy compound feed types. These feed formulations were used to estimate average formulations for high and low protein compound feed for each individual year in the period 2002-2019. For the period 1990-2001 price optimized feed formulations from Schothorst Feed Research were not available. Therefore, the average compound feed formulation was based on 9 dairy compound feed studies performed by Schothorst Feed Research in that period.

CFs of compound feed ingredients were modelled for the major compound feed ingredients that together contributed to 90% of the compound feed composition. A full list is available in the appendix. Individual feed ingredient CF datasets were developed for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the CF results were linearly interpolated. Agri-footprint v5.0 method- ology and background data was used to model the CFs of compound feed ingredients (van Paassen et al., 2019). In this method, trade and pro- duction mixes are calculated based on FAO data to identify the origin countries of crops for the compound feed per year modelled. Input data such as crop yield, fertilizer application, fuel use and land use change were adapted based on national trends from FAO statistics. In total more than 500 crop cultivation CFs were developed. Compound feed in- gredients require subsequent processing and logistics, which was modelled using Agri-footprint v5.0 methodology and background data. Next to variation over time in sourcing of feed ingredients, variation over time in the CF of electricity production and energy efficiency were
included in the CF compound feed datasets.

Direct Land Use Change (dLUC) was assessed using the dLUC methodology prescribed by the PAS-2050-1 (BSI, 2012) and the PEF guidance (European Commission, 2018). However, this study focuses on a trend in CF of raw milk over time rather than a stable point in time. This means that for every individual feed stuff, for every individual year a different GHG emission due to dLUC should be calculated, considering the previous 20-year reference period. A simplified approach was used where the emission due to dLUC was calculated for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the emission due to dLUC was interpolated between the closest reference years.

For purchased dairy cattle, production of diesel, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods CF background datasets using economic allo- cation were selected from Agri-footprint v5.0 and datasets using allo- cation at point of substitution ecoinvent v3.7.1. All selected datasets were copied into the project as system processes to avoid circularity in the process tree. A specification of which background datasets was used for which farm resource is in table B.1.
19: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The CF of a product is the sum of GHG emission and carbon sequestration in a product system, expressed as CO2 equivalents and based on a life cycle assessment (LCA) framework using the single impact category of climate change (ISO, 2018). The raw cow milk CF was determined for the average Dutch dairy farm for the years 1990-2019. The functional unit (FU) is the main product of interest of the analyzed system in quantitative terms and was defined as 1 kg of FPCM at the dairy farm gate in the Netherlands. To explain the results, this study also reports the following FU's for the same period of time: the total Dutch milk delivery at the dairy farm gate in million tons FPCM, and the compound feed ingredients per kg as is at the feed processing gate. The system boundary includes all life cycle stages from cradle until the farm gate at the average Dutch dairy farm. These life cycle stages include the production of resources purchased on the dairy farm (i.e. dairy cattle, compound feed, electricity, natural gas, diesel, artificial fertilizers, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods), and the green- house gas emission related to dairy farm processes (i.e., fertilization, enteric fermentation, manure management and combustion of fossil fuels). Waste treatment of any waste in the life cycle, i.e. silage plastic or capital goods, was excluded from the study except for dairy manure storage and manure application for production of dairy roughage and grazing. Process subdivision was applied to exclude use of farm re- sources for non-dairy purposes. For example, roughage production offered for sale or arable production on farm.
Some life cycle stages result in multiple outputs. Dairy farming, for example, yields milk and cattle for sale, whereas oil pressing of soy

beans yields crude soybean oil and soy-bean expeller. According to the Product Environmental Footprint Category Rules (PEFCR) (EDA, 2018; FEFAC, 2018) and International Dairy Federation (IDF) guidelines for dairy CF (IDF, 2015) economic allocation was used to divide the envi- ronmental impact among multiple outputs in the feed industry and biophysical allocation was used to divide the environmental impact between milk and sold cattle. Manure was treated as a residual product following IDF (2015) and PEFCR baseline recommendations (EDA, 2018), meaning that throughout the life cycle no GHG emission was allocated to manure production.

Including both land use change from cultivation and soil carbon stock changes of the same piece of land results in double counting of biogenic carbon emissions or removals. Including soil carbon stock changes is preferred above inclusion of land use change emissions, because soil carbon stock changes include both increase and decrease of biogenic carbon, whereas land use change from cultivation includes only the decrease of biogenic carbon. Therefore, if data was available, we applied soil carbon stock changes, otherwise we applied land use change and avoided applying both methods simultaneously for any land use in scope. For cultivation of feed ingredients direct land use change from deforestation was included. Direct land use change and soil carbon stock changes resulting from cultivation practices, such as crop rotation or fertilization, were excluded, because not enough information was available for all crops and sourcing regions. Direct land use change was not included for land use on the dairy farm, but instead soil carbon balance was modelled based on annual land use and land use practices. Indirect land use change emission was excluded.

The resulting changes in SOC stocks were converted to CO2 emission or carbon sequestration for the CF. Monthly climate data for the period 1990-2019 was derived for 14 climate zones from the

national weather institute (KNMI). The starting point for SOC stocks was based on a national soil survey from the period 1995-2002 (Visschers et al., 2007). Changes in SOC stocks were simulated for permanent grassland (at least 5 years continuous grassland (CBS, 2022b)), tempo- rary grassland and maize land on sandy and clay soils and a soil depth of 30 cm. Carbon inputs were determined for crop residues and cover crops, manure and compost. Changes in the share of each land use, the type and amount of manure and the application of cover crops together with the climate determined the changes in SOC stocks over time.
2.2.2. Purchased resources

The CF of artificial fertilizers applied and produced in Europe was retrieved from published sources (Brentrup et al., 2016, 2018) for the years 1990, 2006, 2011 and 2014 in the time series and interpolated for intermediate years in the series, between 2014 and 2019 the CF was assumed stable. The CF takes into account the technological changes that occurred during the time series. Since the Netherlands is and has been a net exporter of fertilizer (De Haas and Van Dijk, 2011) all fer- tilizers were assumed to come from the Netherlands with a default transport distance to the dairy farm of 100 km. Details on assumptions and calculations are in Appendix A.

GHG emission from combustion of fuels for electricity production in the Netherlands was estimated for each year by dividing total GHG emission from the electricity sector in the Netherlands by the net elec- tricity produced in the Netherlands (CBS, 2022a). This approach is a simplified version of the "integral approach" as described by Harmelink et al. (2012), which is the basis for national statistics reported by the Central Bureau for Statistics (CBS). However, since data on GHG emis- sion from combustion for 1990-1999 was not available, a simplified method was used to calculate GHG emission from combustion per kilowatt-hour for all years. Appendix C shows a comparison between values used for this study and values from (Harmelink et al., 2012) and CBS. To determine the CF of electricity 5% additional GHG emission was assumed to account for upstream emissions (e.g. mining and transport of fossil fuels) following Edenhofer et al. (2012) and Harmelink et al. (2012).

Compound feed formulations are typically considered as confidential information by the feed industry. Therefore, it is difficult to obtain primary information on compound feed formulation, especially over a period of 30 years. Schothorst Feed Research analyses the market for compound feed ingredients and uses prices and nutritional requirements to optimize feed formulations for different dairy compound feed types. These feed formulations were used to estimate average formulations for high and low protein compound feed for each individual year in the period 2002-2019. For the period 1990-2001 price optimized feed formulations from Schothorst Feed Research were not available. Therefore, the average compound feed formulation was based on 9 dairy compound feed studies performed by Schothorst Feed Research in that period.

CFs of compound feed ingredients were modelled for the major compound feed ingredients that together contributed to 90% of the compound feed composition. A full list is available in the appendix. Individual feed ingredient CF datasets were developed for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the CF results were linearly interpolated. Agri-footprint v5.0 method- ology and background data was used to model the CFs of compound feed ingredients (van Paassen et al., 2019). In this method, trade and pro- duction mixes are calculated based on FAO data to identify the origin countries of crops for the compound feed per year modelled. Input data such as crop yield, fertilizer application, fuel use and land use change were adapted based on national trends from FAO statistics. In total more than 500 crop cultivation CFs were developed. Compound feed in- gredients require subsequent processing and logistics, which was modelled using Agri-footprint v5.0 methodology and background data. Next to variation over time in sourcing of feed ingredients, variation over time in the CF of electricity production and energy efficiency were
included in the CF compound feed datasets.

Direct Land Use Change (dLUC) was assessed using the dLUC methodology prescribed by the PAS-2050-1 (BSI, 2012) and the PEF guidance (European Commission, 2018). However, this study focuses on a trend in CF of raw milk over time rather than a stable point in time. This means that for every individual feed stuff, for every individual year a different GHG emission due to dLUC should be calculated, considering the previous 20-year reference period. A simplified approach was used where the emission due to dLUC was calculated for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the emission due to dLUC was interpolated between the closest reference years.

For purchased dairy cattle, production of diesel, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods CF background datasets using economic allo- cation were selected from Agri-footprint v5.0 and datasets using allo- cation at point of substitution ecoinvent v3.7.1. All selected datasets were copied into the project as system processes to avoid circularity in the process tree. A specification of which background datasets was used for which farm resource is in table B.1.
21: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>
2.1. Goal and scope definition

The CF of a product is the sum of GHG emission and carbon sequestration in a product system, expressed as CO2 equivalents and based on a life cycle assessment (LCA) framework using the single impact category of climate change (ISO, 2018). The raw cow milk CF was determined for the average Dutch dairy farm for the years 1990-2019. The functional unit (FU) is the main product of interest of the analyzed system in quantitative terms and was defined as 1 kg of FPCM at the dairy farm gate in the Netherlands. To explain the results, this study also reports the following FU's for the same period of time: the total Dutch milk delivery at the dairy farm gate in million tons FPCM, and the compound feed ingredients per kg as is at the feed processing gate. The system boundary includes all life cycle stages from cradle until the farm gate at the average Dutch dairy farm. These life cycle stages include the production of resources purchased on the dairy farm (i.e. dairy cattle, compound feed, electricity, natural gas, diesel, artificial fertilizers, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods), and the green- house gas emission related to dairy farm processes (i.e., fertilization, enteric fermentation, manure management and combustion of fossil fuels). Waste treatment of any waste in the life cycle, i.e. silage plastic or capital goods, was excluded from the study except for dairy manure storage and manure application for production of dairy roughage and grazing. Process subdivision was applied to exclude use of farm re- sources for non-dairy purposes. For example, roughage production offered for sale or arable production on farm.
Some life cycle stages result in multiple outputs. Dairy farming, for example, yields milk and cattle for sale, whereas oil pressing of soy

beans yields crude soybean oil and soy-bean expeller. According to the Product Environmental Footprint Category Rules (PEFCR) (EDA, 2018; FEFAC, 2018) and International Dairy Federation (IDF) guidelines for dairy CF (IDF, 2015) economic allocation was used to divide the envi- ronmental impact among multiple outputs in the feed industry and biophysical allocation was used to divide the environmental impact between milk and sold cattle. Manure was treated as a residual product following IDF (2015) and PEFCR baseline recommendations (EDA, 2018), meaning that throughout the life cycle no GHG emission was allocated to manure production.

Including both land use change from cultivation and soil carbon stock changes of the same piece of land results in double counting of biogenic carbon emissions or removals. Including soil carbon stock changes is preferred above inclusion of land use change emissions, because soil carbon stock changes include both increase and decrease of biogenic carbon, whereas land use change from cultivation includes only the decrease of biogenic carbon. Therefore, if data was available, we applied soil carbon stock changes, otherwise we applied land use change and avoided applying both methods simultaneously for any land use in scope. For cultivation of feed ingredients direct land use change from deforestation was included. Direct land use change and soil carbon stock changes resulting from cultivation practices, such as crop rotation or fertilization, were excluded, because not enough information was available for all crops and sourcing regions. Direct land use change was not included for land use on the dairy farm, but instead soil carbon balance was modelled based on annual land use and land use practices. Indirect land use change emission was excluded.
2.2. Life cycle inventory analysis
2.2.1. Data collection

2.2.1.1. Dairy farm processes. Life cycle inventory data was collected on an annual basis for the average Dutch dairy farm for 1990 to 2019, based on Dutch national average dairy farm characteristics in terms of struc- tural variables (i.e. farm size, number of animals, milk production, ni- trogen excretion). The full dataset can be found in the supplementary information of this paper. An overview of the main dairy farm charac- teristics can be found in Table 1.

The average herd composition was calculated from the number of dairy cattle per animal category present in the Netherlands (van Brug- gen et al., 2021) and the number of dairy farms present (CBS, 2022a). The number of sold animals was calculated from the annual replacement rate of dairy cows and the number of calves born per cow per year (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). All youngstock that was born on farm but was not needed for replacement was assumed to be sold. Milk production per cow, including fat and protein content were taken from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) production was
Table 1
Development of main farm characteristics of an average Dutch dairy farm be- tween 1990 and 2019 (CBS, 2022a; van Bruggen et al., 2021; van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020; Wageningen UR, 2022).
a Calculated. b DMI = Dry matter intake.

calculated according to the formula recommended by IDF (IDF, 2015). Feed intake of the dairy herd was calculated from feed intake per animal category and feed type from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020) combined with animal numbers per an- imal category. The feed ration was composed of seven feed types: 1. Fresh grass, 2 grass silage, 3. Maize silage, 4. Co-products, 5. Low pro- tein compound feed, 6. High protein compound feed and 7. Whole milk (for youngstock younger than one year).

Enteric methane emission was calculated from methane emission factors per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and the number of animals per animal category present on farm. For each animal category the share of manure excreted in the slurry or solid manure management system was determined. Methane emission from manure storage was calculated from volatile solids excretion per animal category and the corresponding methane conversion factor for the manure management system. Direct nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calcu- lated from N excretion per animal category in the stable and the weighted emission factor for direct nitrous oxide corresponding with the share of manure excreted in the manure management system. Indirect nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calculated from the percentage of total ammoniacal nitrogen excreted per animal category and nitrogen excretion per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021).

The total area of grassland and maize land used on farm were derived from total mass of dry matter fed to the entire dairy herd, including yield and feed losses (van Dijk et al., 2021) and dry matter yields per hectare from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Soil type distinguished as mineral soil, peaty soil and soil were collected (Arets et al., 2019). Total nitrogen application from manure and artificial fertilizer per hectare were collected from FADN (Wage- ningen UR, 2022). FADN does not distinguish between N application rates on grassland and maize land. Therefore, the same nitrogen appli- cation rate for manure and artificial fertilizer for both grassland and maize land was assumed. Direct nitrous oxide emission from fertilization was calculated from nitrogen application rates for manure and artificial fertilizer and country specific emission factors (Van Dijk et al., 2021; Ruyssenaars et al., 2021) specified per soil type and type of nitrogen applied. For both peaty soils and peat soils the nitrous oxide emission factors for soils were applied.

Manure excreted in the pasture was calculated from N excretion in the pasture per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and animal numbers per animal category. Direct nitrous oxide emissions from manure excretion were calculated from nitrogen excreted in the pasture and a country specific emission factor specified per soil type.

Carbon dioxide and nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was quantified following the approach from Ruyssenaars et al. (2021), using Dutch emission factors based on the level of drainage and organic soil type. A distinction is made between peat soils, having more than 40 cm organic soil layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil, and peaty soils, having a 5-40 cm organic layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil. The share of grassland on organic soils for dairy farming was assumed to be the same as the share of the total area of grassland on organic soils present in the Netherlands, which is derived from Ruys- senaars et al. (2021). The organic soil area on the dairy farm is slightly decreasing over time, because the organic soils area in the Netherlands is oxidating. Nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was calculated based on an emission factor of 4.67 kg N2O-N.kg for peat soils and 4.09 kg N2O-N.kg ha for peaty soils (van der Zee et al., 2021).

The development of SOC stocks in mineral soils was simulated with the soil carbon model RothC (Coleman et al., 1997; Coleman and Jen- kinson, 2014). This is a dynamic model for the conversion of organic carbon in mineral soils. The model considers the effects of soil type, temperature, moisture content and soil cover on the decomposition of organic carbon. The resulting changes in SOC stocks were converted to CO2 emission or carbon sequestration for the CF. Monthly climate data for the period 1990-2019 was derived for 14 climate zones from the

national weather institute (KNMI). The starting point for SOC stocks was based on a national soil survey from the period 1995-2002 (Visschers et al., 2007). Changes in SOC stocks were simulated for permanent grassland (at least 5 years continuous grassland (CBS, 2022b)), tempo- rary grassland and maize land on sandy and clay soils and a soil depth of 30 cm. Carbon inputs were determined for crop residues and cover crops, manure and compost. Changes in the share of each land use, the type and amount of manure and the application of cover crops together with the climate determined the changes in SOC stocks over time.

The raw cow milk CF was determined for the average Dutch dairy farm for the years 1990-2019. The functional unit (FU) is the main product of interest of the analyzed system in quantitative terms and was defined as 1 kg of FPCM at the dairy farm gate in the Netherlands. To explain the results, this study also reports the following FU's for the same period of time: the total Dutch milk delivery at the dairy farm gate in million tons FPCM, and the compound feed ingredients per kg as is at the feed processing gate. The system boundary includes all life cycle stages from cradle until the farm gate at the average Dutch dairy farm. These life cycle stages include the production of resources purchased on the dairy farm (i.e. dairy cattle, compound feed, electricity, natural gas, diesel, artificial fertilizers, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods), and the green- house gas emission related to dairy farm processes (i.e., fertilization, enteric fermentation, manure management and combustion of fossil fuels). Waste treatment of any waste in the life cycle, i.e. silage plastic or capital goods, was excluded from the study except for dairy manure storage and manure application for production of dairy roughage and grazing. Process subdivision was applied to exclude use of farm re- sources for non-dairy purposes. For example, roughage production offered for sale or arable production on farm.
Some life cycle stages result in multiple outputs. Dairy farming, for example, yields milk and cattle for sale, whereas oil pressing of soy

beans yields crude soybean oil and soy-bean expeller. According to the Product Environmental Footprint Category Rules (PEFCR) (EDA, 2018; FEFAC, 2018) and International Dairy Federation (IDF) guidelines for dairy CF (IDF, 2015) economic allocation was used to divide the envi- ronmental impact among multiple outputs in the feed industry and biophysical allocation was used to divide the environmental impact between milk and sold cattle. Manure was treated as a residual product following IDF (2015) and PEFCR baseline recommendations (EDA, 2018), meaning that throughout the life cycle no GHG emission was allocated to manure production.

Including both land use change from cultivation and soil carbon stock changes of the same piece of land results in double counting of biogenic carbon emissions or removals. Including soil carbon stock changes is preferred above inclusion of land use change emissions, because soil carbon stock changes include both increase and decrease of biogenic carbon, whereas land use change from cultivation includes only the decrease of biogenic carbon. Therefore, if data was available, we applied soil carbon stock changes, otherwise we applied land use change and avoided applying both methods simultaneously for any land use in scope. For cultivation of feed ingredients direct land use change from deforestation was included. Direct land use change and soil carbon stock changes resulting from cultivation practices, such as crop rotation or fertilization, were excluded, because not enough information was available for all crops and sourcing regions. Direct land use change was not included for land use on the dairy farm, but instead soil carbon balance was modelled based on annual land use and land use practices. Indirect land use change emission was excluded.
2.2. Life cycle inventory analysis
2.2.1. Data collection

2.2.1.1. Dairy farm processes. Life cycle inventory data was collected on an annual basis for the average Dutch dairy farm for 1990 to 2019, based on Dutch national average dairy farm characteristics in terms of struc- tural variables (i.e. farm size, number of animals, milk production, ni- trogen excretion). The full dataset can be found in the supplementary information of this paper. An overview of the main dairy farm charac- teristics can be found in Table 1.

The average herd composition was calculated from the number of dairy cattle per animal category present in the Netherlands (van Brug- gen et al., 2021) and the number of dairy farms present (CBS, 2022a). The number of sold animals was calculated from the annual replacement rate of dairy cows and the number of calves born per cow per year (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). All youngstock that was born on farm but was not needed for replacement was assumed to be sold. Milk production per cow, including fat and protein content were taken from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) production was
Table 1
Development of main farm characteristics of an average Dutch dairy farm be- tween 1990 and 2019 (CBS, 2022a; van Bruggen et al., 2021; van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020; Wageningen UR, 2022).
a Calculated. b DMI = Dry matter intake.

calculated according to the formula recommended by IDF (IDF, 2015). Feed intake of the dairy herd was calculated from feed intake per animal category and feed type from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020) combined with animal numbers per an- imal category. The feed ration was composed of seven feed types: 1. Fresh grass, 2 grass silage, 3. Maize silage, 4. Co-products, 5. Low pro- tein compound feed, 6. High protein compound feed and 7. Whole milk (for youngstock younger than one year).

Enteric methane emission was calculated from methane emission factors per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and the number of animals per animal category present on farm. For each animal category the share of manure excreted in the slurry or solid manure management system was determined. Methane emission from manure storage was calculated from volatile solids excretion per animal category and the corresponding methane conversion factor for the manure management system. Direct nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calcu- lated from N excretion per animal category in the stable and the weighted emission factor for direct nitrous oxide corresponding with the share of manure excreted in the manure management system. Indirect nitrous oxide emission from manure storage was calculated from the percentage of total ammoniacal nitrogen excreted per animal category and nitrogen excretion per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021).

The total area of grassland and maize land used on farm were derived from total mass of dry matter fed to the entire dairy herd, including yield and feed losses (van Dijk et al., 2021) and dry matter yields per hectare from van Bruggen and Gosseling (2019) and van Bruggen and Gosseling (2020). Soil type distinguished as mineral soil, peaty soil and soil were collected (Arets et al., 2019). Total nitrogen application from manure and artificial fertilizer per hectare were collected from FADN (Wage- ningen UR, 2022). FADN does not distinguish between N application rates on grassland and maize land. Therefore, the same nitrogen appli- cation rate for manure and artificial fertilizer for both grassland and maize land was assumed. Direct nitrous oxide emission from fertilization was calculated from nitrogen application rates for manure and artificial fertilizer and country specific emission factors (Van Dijk et al., 2021; Ruyssenaars et al., 2021) specified per soil type and type of nitrogen applied. For both peaty soils and peat soils the nitrous oxide emission factors for soils were applied.

Manure excreted in the pasture was calculated from N excretion in the pasture per animal category (van Bruggen et al., 2021) and animal numbers per animal category. Direct nitrous oxide emissions from manure excretion were calculated from nitrogen excreted in the pasture and a country specific emission factor specified per soil type.

Carbon dioxide and nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was quantified following the approach from Ruyssenaars et al. (2021), using Dutch emission factors based on the level of drainage and organic soil type. A distinction is made between peat soils, having more than 40 cm organic soil layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil, and peaty soils, having a 5-40 cm organic layer within the upper 80 cm of the soil. The share of grassland on organic soils for dairy farming was assumed to be the same as the share of the total area of grassland on organic soils present in the Netherlands, which is derived from Ruys- senaars et al. (2021). The organic soil area on the dairy farm is slightly decreasing over time, because the organic soils area in the Netherlands is oxidating. Nitrous oxide emission from drained organic soils was calculated based on an emission factor of 4.67 kg N2O-N.kg for peat soils and 4.09 kg N2O-N.kg ha for peaty soils (van der Zee et al., 2021).

The development of SOC stocks in mineral soils was simulated with the soil carbon model RothC (Coleman et al., 1997; Coleman and Jen- kinson, 2014). This is a dynamic model for the conversion of organic carbon in mineral soils. The model considers the effects of soil type, temperature, moisture content and soil cover on the decomposition of organic carbon. The resulting changes in SOC stocks were converted to CO2 emission or carbon sequestration for the CF. Monthly climate data for the period 1990-2019 was derived for 14 climate zones from the

national weather institute (KNMI). The starting point for SOC stocks was based on a national soil survey from the period 1995-2002 (Visschers et al., 2007). Changes in SOC stocks were simulated for permanent grassland (at least 5 years continuous grassland (CBS, 2022b)), tempo- rary grassland and maize land on sandy and clay soils and a soil depth of 30 cm. Carbon inputs were determined for crop residues and cover crops, manure and compost. Changes in the share of each land use, the type and amount of manure and the application of cover crops together with the climate determined the changes in SOC stocks over time.
2.2.2. Purchased resources

The CF of artificial fertilizers applied and produced in Europe was retrieved from published sources (Brentrup et al., 2016, 2018) for the years 1990, 2006, 2011 and 2014 in the time series and interpolated for intermediate years in the series, between 2014 and 2019 the CF was assumed stable. The CF takes into account the technological changes that occurred during the time series. Since the Netherlands is and has been a net exporter of fertilizer (De Haas and Van Dijk, 2011) all fer- tilizers were assumed to come from the Netherlands with a default transport distance to the dairy farm of 100 km. Details on assumptions and calculations are in Appendix A.

GHG emission from combustion of fuels for electricity production in the Netherlands was estimated for each year by dividing total GHG emission from the electricity sector in the Netherlands by the net elec- tricity produced in the Netherlands (CBS, 2022a). This approach is a simplified version of the "integral approach" as described by Harmelink et al. (2012), which is the basis for national statistics reported by the Central Bureau for Statistics (CBS). However, since data on GHG emis- sion from combustion for 1990-1999 was not available, a simplified method was used to calculate GHG emission from combustion per kilowatt-hour for all years. Appendix C shows a comparison between values used for this study and values from (Harmelink et al., 2012) and CBS. To determine the CF of electricity 5% additional GHG emission was assumed to account for upstream emissions (e.g. mining and transport of fossil fuels) following Edenhofer et al. (2012) and Harmelink et al. (2012).

Compound feed formulations are typically considered as confidential information by the feed industry. Therefore, it is difficult to obtain primary information on compound feed formulation, especially over a period of 30 years. Schothorst Feed Research analyses the market for compound feed ingredients and uses prices and nutritional requirements to optimize feed formulations for different dairy compound feed types. These feed formulations were used to estimate average formulations for high and low protein compound feed for each individual year in the period 2002-2019. For the period 1990-2001 price optimized feed formulations from Schothorst Feed Research were not available. Therefore, the average compound feed formulation was based on 9 dairy compound feed studies performed by Schothorst Feed Research in that period.

CFs of compound feed ingredients were modelled for the major compound feed ingredients that together contributed to 90% of the compound feed composition. A full list is available in the appendix. Individual feed ingredient CF datasets were developed for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the CF results were linearly interpolated. Agri-footprint v5.0 method- ology and background data was used to model the CFs of compound feed ingredients (van Paassen et al., 2019). In this method, trade and pro- duction mixes are calculated based on FAO data to identify the origin countries of crops for the compound feed per year modelled. Input data such as crop yield, fertilizer application, fuel use and land use change were adapted based on national trends from FAO statistics. In total more than 500 crop cultivation CFs were developed. Compound feed in- gredients require subsequent processing and logistics, which was modelled using Agri-footprint v5.0 methodology and background data. Next to variation over time in sourcing of feed ingredients, variation over time in the CF of electricity production and energy efficiency were
included in the CF compound feed datasets.

Direct Land Use Change (dLUC) was assessed using the dLUC methodology prescribed by the PAS-2050-1 (BSI, 2012) and the PEF guidance (European Commission, 2018). However, this study focuses on a trend in CF of raw milk over time rather than a stable point in time. This means that for every individual feed stuff, for every individual year a different GHG emission due to dLUC should be calculated, considering the previous 20-year reference period. A simplified approach was used where the emission due to dLUC was calculated for the years 1990, 1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015 and 2019. For intermediate years the emission due to dLUC was interpolated between the closest reference years.

For purchased dairy cattle, production of diesel, pesticides, silage plastic and capital goods CF background datasets using economic allo- cation were selected from Agri-footprint v5.0 and datasets using allo- cation at point of substitution ecoinvent v3.7.1. All selected datasets were copied into the project as system processes to avoid circularity in the process tree. A specification of which background datasets was used for which farm resource is in table B.1.

2.1. Goal and scope definition

The CF of a product is the sum of GHG emission and carbon sequestration in a product system, expressed as CO2 equivalents and based on a life cycle assessment (LCA) framework using the single impact category of climate change (ISO, 2018). The raw cow milk CF was determined for the average Dutch dairy farm for the years 1990-2019. The functional unit (FU) is the main product of interest of the analyzed system in quantitative terms and was defined as 1 kg of FPCM at the dairy farm gate in the Netherlands. Waste treatment of any waste in the life cycle, i.e. silage plastic or capital goods, was excluded from the study except for dairy manure storage and manure application for production of dairy roughage and grazing. Process subdivision was applied to exclude use of farm re- sources for non-dairy purposes. For example, roughage production offered for sale or arable production on farm.
Some life cycle stages result in multiple outputs. Dairy farming, for example, yields milk and cattle for sale, whereas oil pressing of soy

beans yields crude soybean oil and soy-bean expeller. According to the Product Environmental Footprint Category Rules (PEFCR) (EDA, 2018; FEFAC, 2018) and International Dairy Federation (IDF) guidelines for dairy CF (IDF, 2015) economic allocation was used to divide the envi- ronmental impact among multiple outputs in the feed industry and biophysical allocation was used to divide the environmental impact between milk and sold cattle. Manure was treated as a residual product following IDF (2015) and PEFCR baseline recommendations (EDA, 2018), meaning that throughout the life cycle no GHG emission was allocated to manure production.

Therefore, if data was available, we applied soil carbon stock changes, otherwise we applied land use change and avoided applying both methods simultaneously for any land use in scope. Direct land use change was not included for land use on the dairy farm, but instead soil carbon balance was modelled based on annual land use and land use practices. Indirect land use change emission was excluded.

25: The annual CF of raw milk was calculated based on the environ- mental indicator climate change. The life cycle impact assessment was performed in Simapro v9.2.0.1. Global Warming Potentials for a one- hundred-year time horizon (GWP-100) from IPCC (2021) were used to calculate total GHG emission. This implies a GWP-100 of 27.2 kg CO2- eq. per kg biogenic methane and 273 kg CO2 eq. per kg nitrous oxide.
26: To assure the reliability of both the absolute CF and the reduction rate, a sensitivity analysis was performed on the most important meth- odology choices affecting the absolute CF and the trend over the years. The goal was to gain awareness on how variability of activity data and methodological choices (GWP and Tier levels) affects the CF in indi- vidual years and the trendline over the time series.

First, the effect on the CF of using different GWP-100 values for CH4 and N20, i.e. using the 5th assessment report of IPCC instead of the 6th assessment report of IPCC (2021) was investigated. In the 5th assess- ment report of IPCC the GWP-100 including climate carbon feedback was 34 kg CO2-eq. per kg biogenic methane and 298 kg CO2-eq. per kg nitrous oxide. Second, the effect on the CF of using Tier 2 default emission factors from IPCC (IPCC, 2019) instead of tier 3 country spe- cific emission factors for the Netherlands (Tier 3) was investigated. Third, the effect of a higher and lower SOC balance rate on the CF was investigated, since there is limited literature on including the SOC bal- ance to the CF. The higher and lower SOC balance rate was assumed to be 0.3 ton CO2 higher and lower than the average SOC balance in the main results.

Fourth, the sensitivity of the results to variability in the CF of com- pound feed was analyzed in three different ways. The main purpose was to determine the added value of collecting primary data for compound feed when assessing CF changes of raw milk over time. In the first analysis, the composition of compound feed was assumed stable over the entire time period. The composition from 2015 was used for this anal- ysis. In the second analysis the CF of individual feed ingredients was assumed stable over the entire time series. The CF individual feed in- gredients of 2015 was used for this analysis to align as best as possible with Agri-footprint background data. The third analysis was a combi- nation of the first and second sensitivity analysis, which means that both the composition and the CF of the individual compound feed ingredients were assumed stable over the entire time series and based on the 2015 values.
Fifth, to identify the contribution of improvements in agricultural practices on the farm, a sensitivity analysis was set up where all trends

over time in purchased resources were ignored and only trends on the dairy farm were considered. Specifically, this means that: compound feed composition, the CF of individual compound feed ingredients, the CF of artificial fertilizers and the CF of electricity production were assumed to be stable over time. The values from 2015 were used as a reference for this sensitivity analysis.

Sixth, the sensitivity of the results for variables that were based on uncertain assumptions for data on the dairy farm was analyzed. These variables included: the dairy herd replacement rate, the N application rate and the number of hectares of grass and maize production. As data on sold animals leaving the farm were lacking, the number of animals leaving the farm for the main results was calculated based on the replacement rate of dairy cows. However, there are differences in replacement rates reported in different data sources (van Bruggen et al., 2021; Wageningen UR, 2022). Therefore, the effect on the CF of using replacement rates from FADN (Wageningen UR, 2022) was investigated in the sensitivity analysis, instead of the replacement rates used for the main results (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020).

As nitrogen application rates were only available for the average hectare of dairy farmland, in the main results an equal nitrogen fertil- ization rate for grassland and maize land was assumed. In the sensitivity analysis the effect on the CF of this assumption was analyzed using a scenario where, with equal nitrogen fertilization at farm level, no ni- trogen fertilizer was applied to maize land and maximum amount of manure was applied to grassland.

For the main results the amount of hectares of grass and maize production on farm was calculated based on the amount of grass and maize fed and yields of grass and maize per hectare. In the sensitivity analysis the calculated hectares were replaced by the hectares of grass and maize per farm available from FADN.
27: To assure the reliability of both the absolute CF and the reduction rate, a sensitivity analysis was performed on the most important meth- odology choices affecting the absolute CF and the trend over the years. The goal was to gain awareness on how variability of activity data and methodological choices (GWP and Tier levels) affects the CF in indi- vidual years and the trendline over the time series.

First, the effect on the CF of using different GWP-100 values for CH4 and N20, i.e. using the 5th assessment report of IPCC instead of the 6th assessment report of IPCC (2021) was investigated. In the 5th assess- ment report of IPCC the GWP-100 including climate carbon feedback was 34 kg CO2-eq. per kg biogenic methane and 298 kg CO2-eq. per kg nitrous oxide. Second, the effect on the CF of using Tier 2 default emission factors from IPCC (IPCC, 2019) instead of tier 3 country spe- cific emission factors for the Netherlands (Tier 3) was investigated. Third, the effect of a higher and lower SOC balance rate on the CF was investigated, since there is limited literature on including the SOC bal- ance to the CF. The higher and lower SOC balance rate was assumed to be 0.3 ton CO2 higher and lower than the average SOC balance in the main results.

Fourth, the sensitivity of the results to variability in the CF of com- pound feed was analyzed in three different ways. The main purpose was to determine the added value of collecting primary data for compound feed when assessing CF changes of raw milk over time. In the first analysis, the composition of compound feed was assumed stable over the entire time period. The composition from 2015 was used for this anal- ysis. In the second analysis the CF of individual feed ingredients was assumed stable over the entire time series. The CF individual feed in- gredients of 2015 was used for this analysis to align as best as possible with Agri-footprint background data. The third analysis was a combi- nation of the first and second sensitivity analysis, which means that both the composition and the CF of the individual compound feed ingredients were assumed stable over the entire time series and based on the 2015 values.
Fifth, to identify the contribution of improvements in agricultural practices on the farm, a sensitivity analysis was set up where all trends

over time in purchased resources were ignored and only trends on the dairy farm were considered. Specifically, this means that: compound feed composition, the CF of individual compound feed ingredients, the CF of artificial fertilizers and the CF of electricity production were assumed to be stable over time. The values from 2015 were used as a reference for this sensitivity analysis.

Sixth, the sensitivity of the results for variables that were based on uncertain assumptions for data on the dairy farm was analyzed. These variables included: the dairy herd replacement rate, the N application rate and the number of hectares of grass and maize production. As data on sold animals leaving the farm were lacking, the number of animals leaving the farm for the main results was calculated based on the replacement rate of dairy cows. However, there are differences in replacement rates reported in different data sources (van Bruggen et al., 2021; Wageningen UR, 2022). Therefore, the effect on the CF of using replacement rates from FADN (Wageningen UR, 2022) was investigated in the sensitivity analysis, instead of the replacement rates used for the main results (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020).

As nitrogen application rates were only available for the average hectare of dairy farmland, in the main results an equal nitrogen fertil- ization rate for grassland and maize land was assumed. In the sensitivity analysis the effect on the CF of this assumption was analyzed using a scenario where, with equal nitrogen fertilization at farm level, no ni- trogen fertilizer was applied to maize land and maximum amount of manure was applied to grassland.

For the main results the amount of hectares of grass and maize production on farm was calculated based on the amount of grass and maize fed and yields of grass and maize per hectare. In the sensitivity analysis the calculated hectares were replaced by the hectares of grass and maize per farm available from FADN.
28: To assure the reliability of both the absolute CF and the reduction rate, a sensitivity analysis was performed on the most important meth- odology choices affecting the absolute CF and the trend over the years. The goal was to gain awareness on how variability of activity data and methodological choices (GWP and Tier levels) affects the CF in indi- vidual years and the trendline over the time series.

First, the effect on the CF of using different GWP-100 values for CH4 and N20, i.e. using the 5th assessment report of IPCC instead of the 6th assessment report of IPCC (2021) was investigated. In the 5th assess- ment report of IPCC the GWP-100 including climate carbon feedback was 34 kg CO2-eq. per kg biogenic methane and 298 kg CO2-eq. per kg nitrous oxide. Second, the effect on the CF of using Tier 2 default emission factors from IPCC (IPCC, 2019) instead of tier 3 country spe- cific emission factors for the Netherlands (Tier 3) was investigated. Third, the effect of a higher and lower SOC balance rate on the CF was investigated, since there is limited literature on including the SOC bal- ance to the CF. The higher and lower SOC balance rate was assumed to be 0.3 ton CO2 higher and lower than the average SOC balance in the main results.

Fourth, the sensitivity of the results to variability in the CF of com- pound feed was analyzed in three different ways. The main purpose was to determine the added value of collecting primary data for compound feed when assessing CF changes of raw milk over time. In the first analysis, the composition of compound feed was assumed stable over the entire time period. The composition from 2015 was used for this anal- ysis. In the second analysis the CF of individual feed ingredients was assumed stable over the entire time series. The CF individual feed in- gredients of 2015 was used for this analysis to align as best as possible with Agri-footprint background data. The third analysis was a combi- nation of the first and second sensitivity analysis, which means that both the composition and the CF of the individual compound feed ingredients were assumed stable over the entire time series and based on the 2015 values.
Fifth, to identify the contribution of improvements in agricultural practices on the farm, a sensitivity analysis was set up where all trends

over time in purchased resources were ignored and only trends on the dairy farm were considered. 31: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.

factor of milk increased over the period 1990-2019 (see supplementary Enteric methane emission per kg FPCM decreased while allocation data). Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
33: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
36: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
39: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%
