Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
42: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
45: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
47: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.

Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
50: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
53: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
56: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
59: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

Fig. 2. Evolution of the GHG emission from the total Dutch dairy sector between 1990 and 2019 segregated in emission excluding dLUC and SOC balance (blue bar), emissions due to dLUC (red bar) and SOC balance (green bar) expressed in primary y axis. Evolution of the raw milk CF (black line) and total milk delivery in The Netherlands (yellow line) between 1990 and 2019 relative to the year 1990 (secondary y axis).

Emissions from roughage production on farm (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 reduced by 52% towards 2019. This reduction was caused by four drivers. First, the reduction of nitrogen application rates of manure and fertilizer, which led to a decrease in nitrous oxide emission. Second, roughage yields increased, implying a major increase in nitrogen use efficiency. Third, GHG emission from manure excreted in the pasture decreased due to less grazing. Fourth, changes in feed quality and ration adequacy helped increasing feed efficiency. Increasing crop and feed efficiency increased milk production per surface used from 13000 kg FPCM in 1990-18000 kg FPCM in 2019, which reduced the contribution of the subsystem roughage production on farm to the CF.

The GHG emission from the subsystem manure storage and stable (g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) increased by 26% in the period 1990-2019. Increasing GHG emission resulted from increasing excretions of nitrogen and volatile solids and an increasing share of manure excreted in the stable. This again was a result from changing the cows diet to increase productivity and reduced grazing.

GHG emission of purchased resources (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1) reduced by 54% towards 2019 due to three main drivers: (i) the reduction of the CF of compound feed, although considerable interannual variability exists; (ii) the reduction of CF of artificial fer- tilizer and (iii) the decrease of fertilizer use and compound feed per kg of FPCM.

GHG emission of energy production and use (expressed as g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) decreased by 47% towards 2019. This reduction is caused by the lower CF of electricity and higher energy use efficiency per kg FPCM.

Whilst milk production per cow increased from 6003 to 8807 kg milk per cow in the period 1990 to 2019, the replacement rate of cows reduced from 42% to 29% (van Bruggen and Gosseling, 2019, 2020). This resulted in an increase of the allocation factor of milk from 73% in 1990 to 88% in 2019.

The total milk volume of the Dutch dairy sector has increased by 28% since 1990. The vast majority of this increase in milk production took place in the early 2010s, when the EU was preparing to abolish the 'milk quotas' for all member countries. However, after the abolishment in 2015 more stringent phosphate regulations were introduced by the Dutch government (Staatscourant, 2017), which has led to a small decrease in milk production between 2015 and 2019 of -5%.
62: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Evolution of the CF and emission sources

The CF of raw milk produced in the Netherlands in 2019 was 992 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1), while in 1990 it was 1522. This represents a reduction of 35% between 1990 and 2019 (Fig. 1-a). Emission due to dLUC are reported separately in accordance with ISO 14067 (ISO, 2018) and PEFCR dairy (EDA, 2018). To identify which part of the production chain contributed most to the CF, the results were divided in five main subsystems (Fig. 1-b); 1) enteric methane; 2) roughage production on farm; 3) purchased resources; 4) manure storage and stable; and 5) energy use.

Enteric methane was the largest contributing subsystem followed by roughage production on farm, purchased resources, manure storage and stable and energy use. Enteric methane was also the most stable sub- system within the time series, decreasing by 15% per kg FPCM, from 490 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 415 g CO2-eq in 2019 (Fig. 1-b). Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased substantially by 52% per kg FPCM by from 554 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 267 g CO2-eq in 2019. Purchased resources also decreased substantially by 54% per kg FPCM from 283 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 129 CO2-eq in 2019. However, manure storage and stable per kg FPCM increased by 26% from 105 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 131 g CO2-eq in 2019. Energy use for roughage production at farm was the second largest emission source both in 1990 and 2019, followed by purchased resources and manure storage and stable. Emissions from roughage production on farm decreased from 554 to 267 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (-52%), while manure storage and stable increased 105-131 CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 1990 and 2019 respectively (+26%). Emissions from energy use decreased considerably per kg FPCM by 47% from 84 g CO2-eq in 1990 to 45 g CO2-eq in 2019.

GHG emission from dLUC did not change substantially within the time period while SOC balance had high interannual variability and tended to reduce over time. Including dLUC, the CF of raw milk in 1990 was 1652 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019 this had been reduced by 32%

64: Since purchased resources (feed in particular) have a high relative weight in the CF of milk, the role of direct land use change (dLUC) in the CF of the purchased feed materials was investigated separately. Soybean
meal is one of the most important compound feed ingredients. Including dLUC in soybean cultivation increases its CF by four to sixth - fold depending on the time period (Fig. 3).
3.5. SOC balance and emissions from organic soils

SOC balance in mineral soils has had high interannual variation due to weather but there is a tendency to decrease balances over time (Fig. 4). This is reflected by mostly positive balances in the first decade (1990-2000) to mostly negative values in the last two decades (2001-2019).

Emissions from organic soils contributed considerably to the total CF of raw milk. Carbon dioxide emissions from organic soil oxidation contributed to 126-212 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) to annual CFs, while nitrous oxide emissions from mineralization contributed to 16-24 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1). The contribution of both oxidation and mineral- ization emissions tended to decrease towards 2019.
65: <header>3.6.1. Global warming potentials form AR5 vs. AR6 </header>

Using GWP-100 values from AR5 rather than AR6 leads to a 13-17% higher CF for all years. Whilst changing the GWP-100 leads to a sub- stantial difference in the absolute values, the trend between 1990 and 2019 is similar (Fig. 5).

3.6.1.1. Generic vs. country specific emission factors. The choice of emission factors has a substantial impact on the CF results. Using IPCC default emission factors (Tier 2) leads to a 11-14% lower CF for all years when compared to country specific emission factors for the Netherlands (Tier 3) (Fig. 5).
66: Using a high sequestration scenario for the SOC balance of an addi- tional 0.3 ton CO2/ha translates into a reduction of the CF of 12-15 g CO2-eq. FPCM) for all years in the period 1990-2019. Conversely, the low sequestration scenario leads to an increase of 12-15 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) for all years.

The effects of assuming a stable compound feed composition, assuming a stable CF of feed ingredients and a combination of both are shown in appendix E. For the year 2015 the results for all three analyses are exactly the same as the main results. For other individual years differences up to 55 g CO2-eq. can be observed, but overall the trend in

Fig. 3. Evolution of the CF of main feed ingredients between 1990 and 2019. Blue-gradient color bars exclude land use change while orange-gradient color bars include land use change (dLUC). Maize DDGS stands for Distiller's Dried Grains with Soluble.
Fig. 4. Annual SOC balance in mineral soils on an average Dutch dairy farm between 1990 and 2019.

Fig. 5. Sensitivity analysis of the carbon footprint of Dutch raw milk between 1990 and 2019 based on GWP-100 values and choice of emission factors. Dutch specific emission factors (NL EFs) and AR6 (green bar) are the reference in this paper. Alternative scenarios: IPCC default emission factors (IPCC default EFs) in red and AR 5 in blue.
CF is hardly affected by the performed sensitivity analyses.
3.6.4. Contribution of improvements in agricultural practices

When all trends over time in purchased resources were ignored and only trends on the dairy farm were considered, the CF reduction be- tween 1990 and 2019 was 27% (table E.1). Since in the main results the total CF reduction was 35%, the CF of individual compound feed in- gredients, artificial fertilizers and electricity production explains the residual 8% reduction. For 2015 the CF results are identical since this was used as a reference year.

Using a different division of nitrogen fertilization between grassland and maize land resulted in minor differences in annual CFs. Further- more, the reduction trend of the CF of raw milk was hardly affected.

Using FADN data for hectares of grass and maize per farm affected annual CFs up to 13% (appendix E). The reduction trend of the CF was also affected, using FADN data lead to a 5% additional reduction of the CF of raw milk in the period 1990 to 2019.
Using cow replacement data from FADN resulted in minor changes in annual CFs. Furthermore, the reduction trend of the CF of raw milk was hardly affected.

69: Dutch raw milk CF decreased by 35% (per kg FPCM) between 1990 and 2019. There are four determining variables for the CF that evolved in a similar (opposite) trend between 1990 and 2019. (I) Milk produc- tion per cow increased from 6003 kg per cow per year in 1990-8807 kg in 2019 (+47%); (II) milk production per hectare increased from 13000 to 18000 (+38%); (III) feed efficiency increased from 1.1 to 1.25 kg FPCM/kg DM intake (+12%); (IV) the allocation factor of the CF to milk increased from 73 to 88% (+15%).

Several assumptions are important to interpret these results in the right context. The most relevant is that the calculations were made for the average Dutch dairy system. This means that the data used to calculate the CF (van Bruggen et al., 2021) was an average for all the dairy farms in the Netherlands. Taking farm diversity into account can yield different results and possibilities to find solutions to fit each situ- ation (Ledgard et al., 2020; Lizarralde et al., 2014). As an example, the CF of dairy farms located on 100% organic soils would be much higher than the CF of farms located on mineral soils.
The CF reported in this paper ranges from 1613 (in 1994) to 998 (in 2019) g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) which overlaps with the reported values

for western Europe (between 900 and 1530 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1)) (Bava et al., 2014; Flysjo et al., 2011b; Guerci et al., 2014; Mogensen et al., 2015; O'Brien et al., 2015; Robert Kiefer et al., 2015) despite differences in methodological approach, EF and GWP-100 values. Furthermore, the previously reported CF for Dutch raw milk by Tho- massen et al. (2009) of 1320 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 falls within the range of results.

Previous studies have directly compared the CF of milk in different countries and production systems (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Lorenz et al., 2019; Mazzetto et al., 2021). While this can give a general indi- cation of differences in footprint, the scientific validity of direct com- parisons can be questioned due to a multitude of methodological choices. Among these choices: emission factors, allocation method and functional unit are among the most relevant (Flysjo et al., 2011a, 2011b; Yan et al., 2011). This is in line with the sensitivity analysis performed in this paper, which found an impact of up to 27%, but did not affect reduction rate over the time series. The most comprehensive study on this matter concluded that despite the variation within production sys- items their average CF values was 1.0 kg CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) (Lorenz 1 et al., 2019). Furthermore, milk yield and feed efficiency were the main drivers of CF reduction in mixed and pasture-based systems, which is in line with our results. This means that the factors controlling the foot- print can be seen horizontally (comparing systems) and vertically (comparing the same system over time). From our results it is clear that evaluating progress in reducing the CF is possible if consistent data is available. This implies that robust and specialized data systems are powerful tools to assess the current situation but also monitor progress in the future.

The results show that continuous improvement in agricultural practices (increased milk and roughage yields, improved feed efficiency and decreased nitrogen application) has played an important role in reducing the CF of milk over the years. This result is aligned with Capper et al. (2009), Capper and Cady (2020) and Kristensen et al. (2015) who found that increase in milk yield led to a decrease in CF and more effi- cient use of resources in time for the United States and Denmark. Furthermore, this relation is also reported by Gerber et al. (2011) with a global perspective.

Along with the process of improving agricultural practices, the Dutch dairy system has evolved into less grazing and less land devoted to permanent grasslands which decreased carbon sequestration. More specialized dairy system caused the allocation factor to increase towards milk. Enteric methane emissions have shown little reduction over the time series (-15%), while the emissions from manure management have even increased (+26%). Therefore, technological solutions like feed

additives and biodigesters could be considered key in reducing these emissions (aan den Toorn et al., 2021; Beauchemin et al., 2022). Furthermore, the 1.1% annual reduction in the CF of Dutch raw milk, found in this paper is much lower than the 3.1% annual reduction as proposed by SBTI for the commodity pathway for dairy (Science Based Targets, 2020) (SBTI, 2022). Additional efforts are required if the Dutch dairy sector is to play its part in limiting global warming to 1.5 °C.

The reduction of greenhouse gas emission of the total Dutch dairy sector (15%) was lower than the reduction in CF of raw milk (35%). This is explained by an increase in milk production between 1990 and 2019, which opens up the debate about total emissions versus emission per unit of product. On the one hand, The Netherlands has appropriate ag- roecological (soil and climate) and social (cultural, historical, infra- structure) conditions and is therefore one of the strategic places to produce dairy for the world. On the other hand, milk production comes with associated emissions which can be lowered by unit of product but, if production continues to increase it will be more difficult to achieve the emission reduction goals on a sectoral level.

Data availability is one of the main challenges to perform CF studies. While emission factors have a strong weight on the final estimation and its uncertainty, farm activity data (feed, crop rotation, milk production) is scarce and uncertain especially in developing countries. The present study is the first to explicitly consider changes in compound feed composition and the evolution of the CF of feed ingredients over time in the CF of raw milk.

Furthermore, rates of carbon sequestration are difficult to estimate due to the complexity of soil carbon dynamics and lack of standardized and validated methods to account for it. Initiatives such as the LCA guidelines for calculating carbon sequestration in cattle production systems ("C-SEQU," 2021) make steps towards having a common and validated way of accounting for carbon sequestration.

Emissions from organic soils are a relevant source for Dutch agri- culture since 9% of the country's area is covered by this soil type (van Bruggen et al., 2021). Despite the limited share of organic soils of an average farm this study showed that emissions from organic soils contribute considerably to the CF of average raw milk. Therefore, it is relevant to include organic soil emissions in future CF studies.

The relative contribution of the main greenhouse gases (CH4, N20, CO2) to climate change has been under discussion in recent years (Allen et al., 2016; Shine et al., 2007). The distinction between short-lived climate pollutants (SLCP -CH4-) and long-lived climate pollutants (LLCP-CO2-) makes it difficult to express the effect of 1 kg of methane in 'carbon dioxide equivalents', because its relative effect changes depending on the considered time horizon. An alternative metric (GWP*) has been developed, which aims to do more justice to differ- ences between SLCP's and LLCP's (Allen et al., 2018). This new metric is scientifically significant on the global level of atmospheric concentra- tions and for the accurate modelling of future climate scenarios. How- ever, the suitability of this metric to assess emissions on national, chain or farm level can be questioned (Lesschen, 2021).

Aside from focusing on reducing GHG emissions, ruminant systems have several other functions and challenges, such as other environ- mental issues, biodiversity, ecosystems services, landscape value, food security and food quality. All functions should be optimized simultaneously.

While the perspectives from the climate science are clear in the ef- forts needed to decrease emissions towards a scenario of 1.5 °C (IPCC reports) the actions at sector level are less clear. Furthermore, how much can the Dutch dairy sector grow in terms of production and productiv- ity? Will the reported trend in this paper continue? What is the relation
with other ecosystem services beyond climate regulation? It seems clear that a future outlook is needed to search for a balance between deliv- ering nutrition, climate and biodiversity.

for western Europe (between 900 and 1530 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1)) (Bava et al., 2014; Flysjo et al., 2011b; Guerci et al., 2014; Mogensen et al., 2015; O'Brien et al., 2015; Robert Kiefer et al., 2015) despite differences in methodological approach, EF and GWP-100 values. Furthermore, the previously reported CF for Dutch raw milk by Tho- massen et al. (2009) of 1320 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 falls within the range of results.

Previous studies have directly compared the CF of milk in different countries and production systems (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Lorenz et al., 2019; Mazzetto et al., 2021). While this can give a general indi- cation of differences in footprint, the scientific validity of direct com- parisons can be questioned due to a multitude of methodological choices. Among these choices: emission factors, allocation method and functional unit are among the most relevant (Flysjo et al., 2011a, 2011b; Yan et al., 2011). This is in line with the sensitivity analysis performed in this paper, which found an impact of up to 27%, but did not affect reduction rate over the time series. The most comprehensive study on this matter concluded that despite the variation within production sys- items their average CF values was 1.0 kg CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) (Lorenz 1 et al., 2019). Furthermore, milk yield and feed efficiency were the main drivers of CF reduction in mixed and pasture-based systems, which is in line with our results. This means that the factors controlling the foot- print can be seen horizontally (comparing systems) and vertically (comparing the same system over time). From our results it is clear that evaluating progress in reducing the CF is possible if consistent data is available. This implies that robust and specialized data systems are powerful tools to assess the current situation but also monitor progress in the future.

The results show that continuous improvement in agricultural practices (increased milk and roughage yields, improved feed efficiency and decreased nitrogen application) has played an important role in reducing the CF of milk over the years. This result is aligned with Capper et al. (2009), Capper and Cady (2020) and Kristensen et al. (2015) who found that increase in milk yield led to a decrease in CF and more effi- cient use of resources in time for the United States and Denmark. Furthermore, this relation is also reported by Gerber et al. (2011) with a global perspective.

Along with the process of improving agricultural practices, the Dutch dairy system has evolved into less grazing and less land devoted to permanent grasslands which decreased carbon sequestration. More specialized dairy system caused the allocation factor to increase towards milk. Enteric methane emissions have shown little reduction over the time series (-15%), while the emissions from manure management have even increased (+26%). Therefore, technological solutions like feed

additives and biodigesters could be considered key in reducing these emissions (aan den Toorn et al., 2021; Beauchemin et al., 2022). Furthermore, the 1.1% annual reduction in the CF of Dutch raw milk, found in this paper is much lower than the 3.1% annual reduction as proposed by SBTI for the commodity pathway for dairy (Science Based Targets, 2020) (SBTI, 2022). Additional efforts are required if the Dutch dairy sector is to play its part in limiting global warming to 1.5 °C.

The reduction of greenhouse gas emission of the total Dutch dairy sector (15%) was lower than the reduction in CF of raw milk (35%). This is explained by an increase in milk production between 1990 and 2019, which opens up the debate about total emissions versus emission per unit of product. On the one hand, The Netherlands has appropriate ag- roecological (soil and climate) and social (cultural, historical, infra- structure) conditions and is therefore one of the strategic places to produce dairy for the world. On the other hand, milk production comes with associated emissions which can be lowered by unit of product but, if production continues to increase it will be more difficult to achieve the emission reduction goals on a sectoral level.

Data availability is one of the main challenges to perform CF studies. While emission factors have a strong weight on the final estimation and its uncertainty, farm activity data (feed, crop rotation, milk production) is scarce and uncertain especially in developing countries. The present study is the first to explicitly consider changes in compound feed composition and the evolution of the CF of feed ingredients over time in the CF of raw milk.

Furthermore, rates of carbon sequestration are difficult to estimate due to the complexity of soil carbon dynamics and lack of standardized and validated methods to account for it. Initiatives such as the LCA guidelines for calculating carbon sequestration in cattle production systems ("C-SEQU," 2021) make steps towards having a common and validated way of accounting for carbon sequestration.

Emissions from organic soils are a relevant source for Dutch agri- culture since 9% of the country's area is covered by this soil type (van Bruggen et al., 2021). Despite the limited share of organic soils of an average farm this study showed that emissions from organic soils contribute considerably to the CF of average raw milk. Therefore, it is relevant to include organic soil emissions in future CF studies.

The relative contribution of the main greenhouse gases (CH4, N20, CO2) to climate change has been under discussion in recent years (Allen et al., 2016; Shine et al., 2007). The distinction between short-lived climate pollutants (SLCP -CH4-) and long-lived climate pollutants (LLCP-CO2-) makes it difficult to express the effect of 1 kg of methane in 'carbon dioxide equivalents', because its relative effect changes depending on the considered time horizon. An alternative metric (GWP*) has been developed, which aims to do more justice to differ- ences between SLCP's and LLCP's (Allen et al., 2018). This new metric is scientifically significant on the global level of atmospheric concentra- tions and for the accurate modelling of future climate scenarios. How- ever, the suitability of this metric to assess emissions on national, chain or farm level can be questioned (Lesschen, 2021).

Aside from focusing on reducing GHG emissions, ruminant systems have several other functions and challenges, such as other environ- mental issues, biodiversity, ecosystems services, landscape value, food security and food quality. All functions should be optimized simultaneously.

While the perspectives from the climate science are clear in the ef- forts needed to decrease emissions towards a scenario of 1.5 °C (IPCC reports) the actions at sector level are less clear. Furthermore, how much can the Dutch dairy sector grow in terms of production and productiv- ity? Will the reported trend in this paper continue? What is the relation
with other ecosystem services beyond climate regulation? It seems clear that a future outlook is needed to search for a balance between deliv- ering nutrition, climate and biodiversity.
73: Dutch raw milk CF decreased by 35% (per kg FPCM) between 1990 and 2019. There are four determining variables for the CF that evolved in a similar (opposite) trend between 1990 and 2019. (I) Milk produc- tion per cow increased from 6003 kg per cow per year in 1990-8807 kg in 2019 (+47%); (II) milk production per hectare increased from 13000 to 18000 (+38%); (III) feed efficiency increased from 1.1 to 1.25 kg FPCM/kg DM intake (+12%); (IV) the allocation factor of the CF to milk increased from 73 to 88% (+15%).

Several assumptions are important to interpret these results in the right context. The most relevant is that the calculations were made for the average Dutch dairy system. This means that the data used to calculate the CF (van Bruggen et al., 2021) was an average for all the dairy farms in the Netherlands. Taking farm diversity into account can yield different results and possibilities to find solutions to fit each situ- ation (Ledgard et al., 2020; Lizarralde et al., 2014). As an example, the CF of dairy farms located on 100% organic soils would be much higher than the CF of farms located on mineral soils.

(2015) who found that increase in milk yield led to a decrease in CF and more effi- cient use of resources in time for the United States and Denmark. Furthermore, this relation is also reported by Gerber et al. (2011) with a global perspective.

Along with the process of improving agricultural practices, the Dutch dairy system has evolved into less grazing and less land devoted to permanent grasslands which decreased carbon sequestration. More specialized dairy system caused the allocation factor to increase towards milk. Enteric methane emissions have shown little reduction over the time series (-15%), while the emissions from manure management have even increased (+26%). Therefore, technological solutions like feed

additives and biodigesters could be considered key in reducing these emissions (aan den Toorn et al., 2021; Beauchemin et al., 2022). Furthermore, the 1.1% annual reduction in the CF of Dutch raw milk, found in this paper is much lower than the 3.1% annual reduction as proposed by SBTI for the commodity pathway for dairy (Science Based Targets, 2020) (SBTI, 2022). Additional efforts are required if the Dutch dairy sector is to play its part in limiting global warming to 1.5 °C.

The reduction of greenhouse gas emission of the total Dutch dairy sector (15%) was lower than the reduction in CF of raw milk (35%). This is explained by an increase in milk production between 1990 and 2019, which opens up the debate about total emissions versus emission per unit of product. On the one hand, The Netherlands has appropriate ag- roecological (soil and climate) and social (cultural, historical, infra- structure) conditions and is therefore one of the strategic places to produce dairy for the world. On the other hand, milk production comes with associated emissions which can be lowered by unit of product but, if production continues to increase it will be more difficult to achieve the emission reduction goals on a sectoral level.

Data availability is one of the main challenges to perform CF studies. While emission factors have a strong weight on the final estimation and its uncertainty, farm activity data (feed, crop rotation, milk production) is scarce and uncertain especially in developing countries. The present study is the first to explicitly consider changes in compound feed composition and the evolution of the CF of feed ingredients over time in the CF of raw milk.

Furthermore, rates of carbon sequestration are difficult to estimate due to the complexity of soil carbon dynamics and lack of standardized and validated methods to account for it. Initiatives such as the LCA guidelines for calculating carbon sequestration in cattle production systems ("C-SEQU," 2021) make steps towards having a common and validated way of accounting for carbon sequestration.

Emissions from organic soils are a relevant source for Dutch agri- culture since 9% of the country's area is covered by this soil type (van Bruggen et al., 2021). Despite the limited share of organic soils of an average farm this study showed that emissions from organic soils contribute considerably to the CF of average raw milk. Therefore, it is relevant to include organic soil emissions in future CF studies.

The relative contribution of the main greenhouse gases (CH4, N20, CO2) to climate change has been under discussion in recent years (Allen et al., 2016; Shine et al., 2007). The distinction between short-lived climate pollutants (SLCP -CH4-) and long-lived climate pollutants (LLCP-CO2-) makes it difficult to express the effect of 1 kg of methane in 'carbon dioxide equivalents', because its relative effect changes depending on the considered time horizon. An alternative metric (GWP*) has been developed, which aims to do more justice to differ- ences between SLCP's and LLCP's (Allen et al., 2018). This new metric is scientifically significant on the global level of atmospheric concentra- tions and for the accurate modelling of future climate scenarios. How- ever, the suitability of this metric to assess emissions on national, chain or farm level can be questioned (Lesschen, 2021).

Aside from focusing on reducing GHG emissions, ruminant systems have several other functions and challenges, such as other environ- mental issues, biodiversity, ecosystems services, landscape value, food security and food quality. All functions should be optimized simultaneously.

While the perspectives from the climate science are clear in the ef- forts needed to decrease emissions towards a scenario of 1.5 °C (IPCC reports) the actions at sector level are less clear. Furthermore, how much can the Dutch dairy sector grow in terms of production and productiv- ity? Will the reported trend in this paper continue? What is the relation
with other ecosystem services beyond climate regulation? It seems clear that a future outlook is needed to search for a balance between deliv- ering nutrition, climate and biodiversity.
77: Dutch raw milk CF decreased by 35% (per kg FPCM) between 1990 and 2019. There are four determining variables for the CF that evolved in a similar (opposite) trend between 1990 and 2019. (I) Milk produc- tion per cow increased from 6003 kg per cow per year in 1990-8807 kg in 2019 (+47%); (II) milk production per hectare increased from 13000 to 18000 (+38%); (III) feed efficiency increased from 1.1 to 1.25 kg FPCM/kg DM intake (+12%); (IV) the allocation factor of the CF to milk increased from 73 to 88% (+15%).

Several assumptions are important to interpret these results in the right context. The most relevant is that the calculations were made for the average Dutch dairy system. This means that the data used to calculate the CF (van Bruggen et al., 2021) was an average for all the dairy farms in the Netherlands. Taking farm diversity into account can yield different results and possibilities to find solutions to fit each situ- ation (Ledgard et al., 2020; Lizarralde et al., 2014). As an example, the CF of dairy farms located on 100% organic soils would be much higher than the CF of farms located on mineral soils.
The CF reported in this paper ranges from 1613 (in 1994) to 998 (in 2019) g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) which overlaps with the reported values

for western Europe (between 900 and 1530 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-Superscript(1)) (Bava et al., 2014; Flysjo et al., 2011b; Guerci et al., 2014; Mogensen et al., 2015; O'Brien et al., 2015; Robert Kiefer et al., 2015) despite differences in methodological approach, EF and GWP-100 values. Furthermore, the previously reported CF for Dutch raw milk by Tho- massen et al. (2009) of 1320 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM)-1 falls within the range of results.

Previous studies have directly compared the CF of milk in different countries and production systems (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Lorenz et al., 2019; Mazzetto et al., 2021). While this can give a general indi- cation of differences in footprint, the scientific validity of direct com- parisons can be questioned due to a multitude of methodological choices. Among these choices: emission factors, allocation method and functional unit are among the most relevant (Flysjo et al., 2011a, 2011b; Yan et al., 2011). This is in line with the sensitivity analysis performed in this paper, which found an impact of up to 27%, but did not affect reduction rate over the time series. The most comprehensive study on this matter concluded that despite the variation within production sys- items their average CF values was 1.0 kg CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) (Lorenz 1 et al., 2019). Furthermore, milk yield and feed efficiency were the main drivers of CF reduction in mixed and pasture-based systems, which is in line with our results. This means that the factors controlling the foot- print can be seen horizontally (comparing systems) and vertically (comparing the same system over time). From our results it is clear that evaluating progress in reducing the CF is possible if consistent data is available. This implies that robust and specialized data systems are powerful tools to assess the current situation but also monitor progress in the future.

79: This study showed that it is feasible to reliably model the CF of raw milk for the Netherlands over a period of 30 years. The CF of Dutch milk production decreased 35% between 1990 and 2019, from 1522 in 1990 to 992 g CO2-eq. (kg FPCM) in 2019. Both dLUC and SOC balance affected the reduction rate of the CF, which implies that the reduction rate is affected by the scope of the CF study. The emission sources enteric methane, on-farm roughage production on farm, purchased resources and energy use contributed to different extent to a reduction of the CF, whereas the subsystem manure storage and stable contributed to an increase of the CF over time. SOC balance was variable and had a limited effect on the CF results, but deteriorated over time. The contribution of dLUC to the CF was stable over time. Organic soils emissions are relevant to include as they contribute significant to total GHG emissions an CF of raw milk. Higher milk and roughage yields, improved feed efficiency and less fertilizer use were the main drivers for the reduction, whereas a reduction over time of grazing hours and permanent grassland had increasing effects on the CF of Dutch raw milk. Methodological choices affect the absolute level of the CF by up to 27%, but the impact on the reduction rate over time was negligible. This confirms that the achieved reduction in CF of Dutch raw milk over time is robust. Comparing the CF of raw milk that have differences in data quality and methodology should not be performed without taking these differences into account.
Total emissions from Dutch dairy sector reduced less (15%) than the CF per kg of raw milk (35%), because total raw Dutch milk production increased significantly.

In order to achieve climate targets as indicated by SBTi, the annual reduction rate needs to be increased. Special attention is needed for the reduction of GHG emissions from enteric fermentation and manure storage. Moreover, to achieve a net zero CF the negative SOC balance on Dutch dairy farms needs to be transformed into a positive SOC balance again. However, this study shows that the potential to mitigate the CF of raw milk through carbon sequestration is limited and much more carbon should be added and maintained in the soil in order to have a large impact. The main challenge for the future is to find a balanced set of measures to integrally reduce all the sources of emissions within the carbon footprint of milk.

85: <header>Appendix A. CF of nitrogen fertilizers production </header>

The scope of the emissions generated for fertilizer production followed Brentrup et al. (Brentrup et al., 2016, 2018). This includes emissions from cradle-to-factory-gate in Europe, taking into account the importation of raw materials, production of intermediates and the finishing process combining the materials into a final product. This CF accounts for all emissions.

From the proportion of different fertilizers used we decided to focus on calcium ammonium nitrate (CAN) since it has been the most fertilizer used in The Netherlands during the time series (Fig. A.1).
